title: The potential for linked administrative data to provide household and family information | Stats NZ
content:
## The potential for linked administrative data to provide household and family information
04 April 2018, 10:45am
Administrative data, the data collected for purposes other than official statistics, offer exciting new opportunities to source information about people, dwellings, households, and families.
In the future, Stats NZ may use alternative data sources to produce the statistics that currently come from census data. How accurately could these alternative sources replicate census data on households and families? This was the question we explored in a recent investigation.

### What are household and family statistics?
Information about New Zealand’s households and families is of great importance to many government agencies and organisations. At present Stats NZ only produces household and family information from household surveys and the five-yearly census. Census household and family information includes counts of households, household-size distribution, and information about the relationships between individuals within households. This information is used to develop and evaluate government policies, such as income support and social housing.
A big gap for researchers using Stats NZ’s Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI) is the lack of household and family data, except in the census years.

### Can we construct households and families from admin data?
We used linked admin data available in the IDI to create households and families that could be compared against 2013 Census data on households and families.
We constructed households by using address information from seven sources of address information. All individuals living at the same address were considered to be part of the same household.
To combine people into families we needed data linking parents to children, and linking relationship partners. This family relationship information came from birth registrations, marriage and civil union registrations, family assistance and benefits data, and visa applications. People who we identified as family members through one of these sources, and who were living at the same address, were considered to be part of the same family.

### How good is admin data for households and families?
We found mixed results. On an aggregate level, admin households looked similar to census households – the overall count of households and the distribution of people per household were both comparable. However, when we looked at the individuals placed into each household, only half of admin households had exactly the same individuals as census households. This disparity would create problems with calculating variables for these households, such as household income.
When family information was available from admin sources it tended to be correct. Unfortunately, family information was not available for many people in the population, which meant we could only identify 60 percent of census families. This lack of coverage means it is not currently possible to produce family statistics using admin data.
Future work in this area requires effort to improve the available admin data sources and improvements to some aspects of data coding.
